content:
From Anemic Data Models to Rich Domain Models — Without Sacrificing Performance

10 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Domain-Driven Design tells us that the domain model is the heart of the application. Entities encapsulate business rules. Value objects have no identity. Aggregates enforce consistency boundaries. And the database? It’s an infrastructure detail.
But then you open Entity Framework Core and see public int Id { get; set; }, parameterless constructors, and DbSet<Order> directly exposed to controllers. The domain model becomes a data model. Business logic leaks into services. And the "repository" is just a thin wrapper around DbContext.
This article shows you how to map real DDD concepts — aggregates, value objects, entities, domain events — to EF Core without compromising encapsulation, performance, or testability.
The Core Conflict: DDD vs. EF Core
Press enter or click to view image in full size

EF Core 8+ bridges most of these gaps. The key is knowing which feature maps to which DDD concept.
Concept 1: Entities — Identity and…
